task: Answers must cite our current internal HR policy docs, which change quarterly.
rationale: Private, large, changing knowledge that needs citations and instant updates. Facts belong in retrieval, not weights; fine-tuning would half-memorize and go stale.

task: Every reply must follow our house JSON schema and clipped support-desk tone, on one narrow ticket-triage task we run a million times a day.
rationale: Narrow, stable, high-volume task needing consistent format/behavior. Form-and-behavior is exactly what weights encode; volume amortizes the training cost.

task: The model should refuse off-topic requests and adopt a warmer brand voice across all features.
rationale: Behavior, tone, and policy can change in the system prompt first: fast, cheap, reversible, no infra. Always the first move.

task: Summaries are missing facts from a 4,000-page product manual the model has never seen.
rationale: Knowledge the model lacks and that exceeds the context window. Retrieve the relevant passages; this is missing knowledge, not missing skill or style.

task: We want a 7B model to match our frontier model on one classification task, served 50x cheaper at volume.
rationale: Distill a proven, narrow capability into a smaller cheaper model. The task is stable and the volume justifies the asset; this is the economic engine of customization.

task: Make the assistant ask one clarifying question before long tasks.
rationale: A workflow/behavior tweak expressible as an instruction. Reach for prompting first; it is reversible and needs no data or infra.

task: Inject our 12,000 product SKUs and live prices so the model can quote them.
rationale: The classic anti-pattern is fine-tuning to inject facts; prices change and must be attributable. Retrieve current SKUs; never bake volatile facts into weights.
